openai_trial_gate.recommendation: OpenAI remains T1 mandatory reassessment — do not score until Day 1 voir dire questions and Day 2 opening arguments are retrieved. top_entities.name: OpenAI
top_entities.key_trigger: Trial Day 1 jury selection begins April 27 in Oakland federal court — Musk v. Altman goes live; April 23 PBC conversion memo strips nonprofit of controlling stake; two remaining claims: breach of charitable trust + unjust enrichment; $150B potential remedy directed to charitable arm
top_entities.news_summary: Jury selection began April 27 in the Musk v. Altman trial at Oakland federal court. The lawsuit centers on whether OpenAI's leadership betrayed its founding charitable mission when converting from nonprofit to for-profit structure. Two claims remain after Musk dropped fraud claims April 25: unjust enrichment and breach of charitable trust. Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers will make final remedial decisions; the jury serves in advisory capacity only. Opening arguments expected April 28. Both Musk and Altman, plus Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella and Shivon Zilis, are expected on the stand. Separately: an April 23 OpenAI internal memo revealed that the OpenAI Foundation (nonprofit) is being stripped of its controlling stake in the PBC, becoming a minority non-voting interest holder. This is the primary governance change that the trial is adjudicating. The trial has been described as a 'test case for AI ethics' and nonprofit governance accountability.
top_entities.rationale: Trial Day 1 is an in-window event (April 27). The proceeding directly tests OpenAI's INT dimension — whether the organization maintained fidelity to its stated charitable mission during structural conversion. The April 23 PBC conversion that strips nonprofit control is the largest organizational governance change in OpenAI's history and directly affects SYS/INT scoring. Assessed April 25 (2 days ago, staleness 5 pts) — new material from trial opening and PBC memo justifies re-queue. AI Labs importance 10 pts. Trial outcome is a pending inflection point with potential composite impact in both directions.
top_entities.assessor_action: GATE: Retrieve Day 1 voir dire questions and Day 2 opening arguments before scoring. Primary scoring anchors: (a) April 23 PBC conversion — nonprofit loses controlling stake is an INT/SYS anchor (organizational mission drift institutionalized); (b) breach of charitable trust claim as quasi-judicial framing for INT dimension; (c) Musk's own fraud dismissal as partial signal that core charitable mission deviation, not individual fraud, is the primary issue. Score composite movement against April 25 hold at 31.3. Assess whether trial itself (as accountability mechanism, INT positive) partially offsets structural governance failure (INT negative).

top_entities.name: Netherlands
top_entities.key_trigger: Dutch Senate PASSES two-tier asylum system April 21 — law divides refugees into A-status (persecution) and B-status (war/climate) with B-status denied family reunification rights for 2 years; April 25 violent anti-asylum protests prompt Loosdrecht to cut asylum intake; April 10 Netherlands elected Iran to UN human rights body (ECOSOC); never assessed; composite 90.9
top_entities.news_summary: MAJOR IN-WINDOW EVENTS: (1) April 21, 2026: The Dutch Senate approved the two-tier asylum system, creating A-status (fleeing personal persecution) and B-status (fleeing war or climate change). B-status holders face a 2-year wait before even applying for family reunification, must have suitable housing for entire family, and must demonstrate sufficient income — restrictions that rights groups including RefugeeHelp and the Dutch Council for Refugees call a 'lottery of rights' and a violation of fundamental human rights. Amnesty International and the European Council on Refugees and Exiles (ECRE) urged delay. (2) April 25: Violent anti-asylum protests in Loosdrecht prompted authorities to cut the asylum seeker capacity from 110 to 70 and delay opening until May 6 — government yielding to violent protest pressure to reduce humanitarian intake. (3) April 10: Netherlands voted at ECOSOC to elect Iran to a UN human rights policy body — directly contradicting stated human rights leadership. Previously captured in Apr 26 scan but the April 21 Senate passage is a new, decisive in-window legislative event. Never assessed.
top_entities.rationale: The April 21 Senate passage of the two-tier asylum system is a major legislative event that converts the previously-tracked policy intent into binding law. This is substantially stronger than the Amnesty report or ECOSOC vote alone — it is an enacted legislative change removing previously recognized refugee rights (family reunification) for a specific class of protection seekers. This is a direct EQU/INT failure event: formally codifying unequal protection based on reason-for-flight category. The April 25 government capitulation to violent protests (reducing intake) adds an ACC dimension signal. Countries importance 12 pts. Staleness 25 pts. Never assessed. Strong ceiling correction candidate (composite 90.9).
top_entities.assessor_action: First evidence-based assessment. Score: (a) ceiling methodology audit for composite 90.9 (country rank 8, paired with Germany 90.9); (b) April 21 two-tier asylum system as primary EQU anchor — codified differentiation in refugee rights based on flight reason, with family reunification denial as the specific harm; (c) April 10 ECOSOC Iran vote as INT signal (institutional action contradicts stated human rights leadership); (d) April 25 Loosdrecht capitulation as ACC signal (yields to violent pressure to reduce humanitarian intake); (e) Wilders coalition general asylum restrictions as SYS context. Calibration reference: Germany (90.9, never assessed) is the natural ceiling pair — assess both on Night 9.

top_entities.name: Palantir AI
top_entities.key_trigger: NEW April 24 event (first confirmed in today's scan window): The Intercept reveals Palantir has $130M IRS contract for 'massive-scale data mining' of taxpayer records, ACA data, FinCEN financial crimes data, cryptocurrency wallet data — a scope expansion from immigration surveillance into population-level financial surveillance; Employee 'descent into fascism' discussions April 25 (new internal dissent signal); composite 19.9 within 0.1 of 20-band boundary
top_entities.news_summary: NEW IN-WINDOW EVIDENCE (distinct from April 26 scan): The Intercept published April 24 revealing that Palantir has earned $130M from IRS Criminal Investigation since 2018 for its Lead and Case Analytics platform, which aggregates: individual tax returns and tax forms, Affordable Care Act data, bank statements, FinCEN financial crimes data, 'all available' Treasury Department data, and dark web cryptocurrency wallet data. The platform allows searching 'millions of records with thousands of links.' Privacy experts call this 'secondary data abuse' — data collected for tax purposes being used for a sprawling surveillance apparatus. This is categorically different from the immigration enforcement contracts documented in the April 26 scan — it represents Palantir as a general-purpose government surveillance infrastructure provider, not limited to immigration. Separately April 25: Business Story documented Palantir employees discussing the company's 'descent into fascism,' with staff asking 'are we the baddies?' after internal questions about ICE work were auto-deleted from company Slack. Activist protests at Palantir's Manhattan offices were documented by Business and Human Rights Centre. Composite 19.9 is within 0.1pt of the 20-band boundary. Assessed April 26 — new material justifies same-day re-queue under volatility protocol.
top_entities.rationale: The IRS/$130M data mining story is a NEW event — not captured in the April 26 scan (which focused on EFF April 25 letter and congressional non-response deadline). The Intercept story was published April 24 and documents a scope of government data integration that goes beyond immigration surveillance into general financial surveillance. This is a BND dimension event: Palantir is not just facilitating targeted immigration enforcement — it is the infrastructure for broad-based population financial surveillance across IRS, ACA, FinCEN, and Treasury data. This changes the composite assessment materially. Employee dissent ('auto-deleted Slack questions,' 'descent into fascism') is an INT governance signal. Composite 19.9 within 0.1pt of 20 boundary — maximum volatility. Re-queue justified under the volatility protocol for entities within 1pt of band boundary with new material evidence.
top_entities.assessor_action: Re-assessment building on April 26 baseline. NEW scoring anchor: IRS $130M contract for taxpayer data mining across ACA, FinCEN, Treasury data — this is qualitatively distinct from immigration enforcement (which affects non-citizen population) as it encompasses general US taxpayer population surveillance. Score: (a) IRS data mining as BND primary — deliberate design of cross-agency surveillance infrastructure affecting general population; (b) auto-deleted employee questions as INT/ACC signal (suppression of internal accountability); (c) activist protests + employee dissent as external accountability pressure documentation. Assess whether composite should remain at 19.9 or move given expanded scope of harm facilitation.

top_entities.name: Texas
top_entities.key_trigger: April 24: 5th Circuit Court allows Texas SB4 immigration law to take effect after ruling plaintiffs lack standing — law lets state police arrest people suspected of illegal border entry and order removal to Mexico; Houston sanctuary ordinance gutted April 22 under Abbott funding threat ($110M+ frozen); Texas holds 18,734 ICE detainees — more than double any other state
top_entities.news_summary: Two major in-window events for Texas: (1) April 24: The conservative 5th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals ruled plaintiffs lacked standing to challenge SB4, allowing the 2023 law to take effect. SB4 lets state police arrest individuals suspected of illegal border crossing and allows magistrate judges to order removal to Mexico — effectively creating a state-level parallel deportation system. Immigration rights groups called the ruling a 'reign of fear and targeted profiling.' (2) April 22: Houston City Council voted 13-4 to gut its ordinance limiting ICE cooperation, after Governor Abbott froze more than $110M in public safety grants. The amended ordinance removed language specifying that administrative warrants (not signed by a judge) do not justify detention — meaning Houston police can now detain individuals on Palantir-generated ICE administrative warrants alone. Texas AG Ken Paxton had sued the Mayor and city council over the original ordinance. Texas currently holds 18,734 ICE detainees — more than double any other state. Never assessed; composite 15.3, Critical band.
top_entities.rationale: Two independent major negative events in window: SB4 court victory enables state-level parallel immigration enforcement with racial profiling risk; Houston ordinance gutting documents government funding coercion as a mechanism to override local human rights protections. Both events directly affect EQU (discriminatory enforcement targeting specific populations), ACC (removal of judicial warrant requirement), and BND (state as facilitator of harm). Texas is the 5th-largest entity never assessed (rank 48, composite 15.3) in the US States index with three direct in-window events. The state's role as ICE's primary detention hub (18,734 detainees) is now formally supported by new state law and policy changes. US States importance 3 pts. Staleness 25 pts.
top_entities.assessor_action: First baseline assessment. Score: (a) SB4 activation (April 24) as primary EQU/BND anchor — state law authorizing racially targeted arrests and summary deportation without federal oversight; (b) Houston ordinance gutting (April 22) as ACC signal — financial coercion used to override local accountability infrastructure; (c) 18,734 ICE detainees (highest in nation) as BND scope indicator; (d) verify current composite 15.3 against Critical-band evidence cluster. Texas has virtually no positive SYS indicators to offset: state legislature actively dismantling civil rights protections, trans healthcare bans, voting restrictions, education censorship active.

top_entities.name: Israel
top_entities.key_trigger: Amnesty International 2026 Annual Report (April 20) formally documents Israel maintaining 'genocide against Palestinians in Gaza' and 'system of apartheid over Palestinians' — institutional finding that upgrades evidence quality for INT/BND dimensions; Gaza crossings closed since Feb 28 (60+ days of total aid blockade); Doctors Without Borders, Oxfam, IRC + 33 other humanitarian organizations banned from Gaza
top_entities.news_summary: Two compounding in-window events: (1) Amnesty International's 2026 Annual Report (April 20, 2026) formally states Israel has maintained 'its genocide against Palestinians in Gaza, despite the October 2025 ceasefire agreement, and its system of apartheid over Palestinians.' This is a designated institutional human rights finding — not media coverage — and significantly upgrades the evidence quality for INT and BND scoring. Amnesty also documents Israel 'increasingly allowing or encouraging settlers to attack and terrorize Palestinians with impunity.' (2) Gaza's total aid blockade has now extended 57+ days (all crossings closed since Feb 28). Israel has banned over 36 humanitarian organizations from operating in Gaza, including Doctors Without Borders, Oxfam, the Norwegian Refugee Council, CARE International, and the International Rescue Committee. This represents active dismantling of the humanitarian response infrastructure in a conflict zone. Assessed April 21 (6 days). Combined with the April 2026 Security Council debate captured in the April 26 scan.
top_entities.rationale: The Amnesty 2026 Annual Report is a substantive institutional finding event — distinct from prior press coverage — that formally documents genocide and apartheid findings. The banning of 36+ humanitarian organizations from Gaza (including MSF and Oxfam) is a new, quantifiable BND event: active prevention of organized humanitarian response. The combination of these two events justifies a re-queue even though assessed 6 days ago (April 21). Countries importance 12 pts. Evidence quality is now at institutional-finding level (UN + Amnesty + Security Council all documented within window).
top_entities.assessor_action: Re-assessment. Score: (a) Amnesty 2026 genocide/apartheid finding as INT/BND anchor — institutional finding upgrades evidence tier from journalistic to quasi-judicial; (b) 36+ humanitarian organization bans as BND dimension — deliberate dismantling of humanitarian response infrastructure; (c) 57+ day total aid blockade as ACT failure; (d) ceasefire violation count (2,400+) as ongoing INT breach. Composite 8.8 — confirm floor appropriateness. Any positive signal (aid crossing reopening, humanitarian organization readmission) would trigger upward review. No such signal present in window.

top_entities.name: Houston
top_entities.key_trigger: April 22: Houston City Council votes 13-4 to gut sanctuary ordinance under $110M+ funding threat from Texas governor; original ordinance (April 8, 12-5 vote) revoked due to state coercion; civil rights groups say council 'approved gutting of policy limiting ICE cooperation'; Texas AG had filed lawsuit against mayor and city council
top_entities.news_summary: Two in-window events: (1) April 8: Houston City Council voted 12-5 to adopt an ordinance limiting ICE cooperation — specifically stating that ICE administrative warrants (not signed by judges) do not justify prolonging detention, and eliminating the 30-minute wait for federal agents. (2) April 22: Houston City Council voted 13-4 to gut the ordinance after Governor Abbott froze $110M+ in public safety grants. The amended ordinance removed the judicial warrant protection — restoring the ability of Houston police to detain individuals on Palantir-generated administrative warrants alone. Civil rights groups stated the council 'approved gutting of policy limiting ICE cooperation.' This is a complete reversal of a human rights protection achieved 14 days earlier, under direct financial coercion from the state government. Texas AG Ken Paxton had filed a lawsuit against the Mayor and city council. Never assessed; composite 43.8, Functional band.
top_entities.rationale: A complete reversal of a human rights protection within 14 days, under state financial coercion, is a direct ACC dimension event — the city government demonstrated it cannot maintain accountability structures against superior government pressure. The original ordinance passage (April 8) and its revocation (April 22) together document a failed attempt at local human rights protection. This is categorically different from a city that never passed such a protection — Houston passed it, then was coerced into reversing it. The April 22 reversal is the primary scoring anchor. Never assessed. US Cities importance 5 pts. Staleness 25 pts.
top_entities.assessor_action: First baseline assessment. Score: (a) April 22 ordinance reversal as primary ACC anchor — human rights protection reversed under financial coercion; (b) Texas AG lawsuit against mayor and council as INT signal (state using legal apparatus to override local rights protections); (c) composite 43.8 Functional band — assess whether the failed protection attempt and reversal warrants downward movement given the ACT-reversal pattern. Note: Houston has genuine positive indicators (healthcare sector, diverse population, port commerce) that anchor the SYS floor; the reversal is the acute negative event, not a structural institutional failure.

top_entities.name: United States
top_entities.key_trigger: April 24: EO 14398 anti-DEI federal contractor rule takes effect — FAR 52.222-90 clause required in all new contracts; April 22: Houston sanctuary ordinance coercion documents federal-state-local cascade of immigration enforcement; Amnesty 2026 Annual Report documents US as repressing protests and civil society; DEI enforcement window runs through May 25
top_entities.news_summary: Three in-window events: (1) April 24-25: EO 14398 (Anti-DEI for Federal Contractors) begins implementation — FAR clause 52.222-90 now required in all new federal contracts, prohibiting 'racially discriminatory DEI activities.' Failure to comply triggers contract cancellation, False Claims Act liability, and debarment. Legal challenges filed by higher education and minority trade associations. (2) April 22: Houston sanctuary ordinance reversal under state financial coercion documents the national pattern of immigration enforcement cascading from federal to state to local levels. (3) Amnesty 2026 Annual Report: US listed as repressing protests through violent force, along with Afghanistan, China, Egypt, India, Iran, Kenya, and Venezuela. National Guard deployed to cities with majority Black mayors. Assessed April 17 (10 days). Composite 35.5 in Developing band.
top_entities.rationale: EO 14398 implementation (April 24) is a major structural policy event — the largest rollback of DEI protections for federal contractors in US history, with FCA enforcement teeth. The combination of: (a) DEI rollback as EQU/SYS negative; (b) sanctuary city coercion pattern (documented through Houston) as ACC negative; (c) protest repression in Amnesty 2026 as INT/EQU negative; represents three independent structural indicators. Assessed April 17 (10 days). Countries importance 12 pts.
top_entities.assessor_action: Re-assessment. Score: (a) EO 14398 implementation as EQU/SYS anchor — institutionalized rollback of equity protections with FCA enforcement; (b) sanctuary city coercion cascade as ACC anchor — federal/state financial coercion overriding local human rights protections; (c) Amnesty protest repression finding as INT signal. Assess whether composite 35.5 should move given the cumulative structural policy changes documented in April 2026. Consider that EO 14398 affects 800K+ federal contractors — scope is national.

The April 18 progress (COVM operationalized, 477 prisoner release commitment) remains the primary positive ACT signal, but the Amnesty finding documents that atrocities occurred specifically in Goma and Bukavu — the same cities over which the ceasefire mechanism was negotiated. Assessed April 24 (3 days).
top_entities.rationale: The Amnesty 2026 institutional finding for DRC (M23 killings + Rwanda complicity) is a new event — not a prior scan capture — that upgrades the evidence quality for the BND/INT dimensions being scored against the April 18 COVM mechanism. The assessor must now weigh a formal institutional atrocity finding against a ceasefire mechanism that was signed by parties documented as committing those atrocities. This is a material scoring consideration. Assessed April 24 (0 staleness), but new evidence from Amnesty justifies T1 re-queue. Countries importance 12 pts.
top_entities.assessor_action: Re-assessment. Score: (a) Amnesty 2026 M23 killings/torture finding as primary BND anchor — institutional finding of atrocities committed by signatory to the April 18 ceasefire mechanism; (b) April 18 COVM operationalization remains a positive ACT signal — formal commitment infrastructure now exists; (c) assess whether commitment credibility is undermined by the Amnesty atrocity documentation. The key question: does COVM operationalization represent genuine institutional progress (partial ACT positive) or a diplomatic facade covering ongoing atrocities (INT negative)?

top_entities.name: Canada
top_entities.key_trigger: Never assessed; composite 79.6; countries Established band ceiling sprint candidate; staleness 25 pts; no acute in-window event; natural pair with Ireland (79.6, never assessed)
top_entities.news_summary: No acute in-window event identified for Canada in April 25-27 window. Sector sweep: Canada captured in Amnesty 2026 annual report in European/Western pattern context. Canada is a natural ceiling sprint candidate (79.6, rank 11, never assessed) paired with Ireland (79.6, rank 12, never assessed). The Canadian federal election in April 2025 and subsequent Liberal minority government policy positions have not generated a specific compassion-relevant in-window event. Staleness drives this queue position.
top_entities.rationale: Staleness rotation. Canada (79.6, rank 11, never assessed) is the highest-importance never-assessed country outside the currently active ceiling sprints. Countries importance 12 pts. Staleness 25 pts. The pipeline should begin the 79.6 countries ceiling sweep (Canada, Ireland) as the 90+ band sweep completes.
top_entities.assessor_action: First baseline assessment. Score evidence-based ceiling audit for composite 79.6. Focus on: (a) universal healthcare and social programs (positive SYS); (b) indigenous rights record (Calls to Action implementation progress — partial positive ACT against documented shortfalls); (c) immigration and refugee policy (historically positive EQU, though recent processing backlog concerns); (d) climate policy implementation (positive SYS); (e) residential school legacy and reparative progress (BND/ACC historical record). Calibration: Canada (79.6) sits in Established band — the evidence should support approximately 70-85pt range.

top_entities.name: Ireland
top_entities.key_trigger: Never assessed; composite 79.6; natural pair with Canada (79.6, never assessed); Ireland's housing crisis and Direct Provision legacy relevant to EQU/ACT dimensions; no acute in-window event
top_entities.news_summary: No acute in-window event identified for Ireland in April 25-27 window. Ireland is the natural paired ceiling assessment with Canada at 79.6. Ireland's notable recent developments include: passage of Work Permits Bill 2024 (improved labor migration rights — positive EQU), Direct Provision phase-out progress (positive ACT), ongoing housing crisis with asylum seeker accommodation shortfalls (negative EQU/ACT). No specific in-window event identified.
top_entities.rationale: Staleness rotation. Ireland (79.6, rank 12, never assessed) is the natural paired assessment with Canada. Both sit at the same composite, same band. Countries importance 12 pts. Staleness 25 pts.
top_entities.assessor_action: First baseline assessment. Score evidence-based ceiling audit for composite 79.6. Focus on: (a) Direct Provision phase-out (positive ACT); (b) housing crisis and asylum seeker shortfalls (negative EQU/ACT); (c) reproductive rights expansion (positive EQU); (d) LGBTQ+ rights leadership (positive EQU); (e) historical Catholic institutional abuse legacy and reparative actions (BND/ACC record). Ireland likely sits at the high end of the Established band given its progressive rights trajectory.

top_entities.name: xAI/Grok
top_entities.key_trigger: Floor-escalation protocol (Tier H4) overdue — 10+ confirmed negative evidence tiers; California cease-and-desist compliance status unchanged; French criminal probe (April 20 Musk summoned); Palantir-xAI finance partnership (new sector signal); floor-escalation methodology must be applied before next index build
top_entities.news_summary: No new acute xAI event on April 27. Sector sweep: xAI has partnered with Palantir Technologies for AI in finance — a new relationship that compounds xAI's institutional accountability exposure (two entities with documented human rights concerns in a commercial partnership). California AG cease-and-desist compliance status from January 2026 remains unchanged — xAI restricted Grok image editing to paying subscribers but no substantive remediation. French criminal probe is ongoing (April 20 hearing confirmed, Musk summoned). The floor-escalation methodology (Tier H4) has been flagged for four consecutive scans — this is an overdue internal methodology action, not a new evidence event.
top_entities.rationale: The Tier H4 floor-escalation protocol is overdue (flagged nights 5-8, now Night 9). While there is no new in-window negative event justifying a score reduction (the floor is already 2.2), the assessor must apply the floor-escalation methodology to document whether 2.2 is the correct minimum expression or whether a permanent floor designation is warranted. The Palantir-xAI partnership is a minor new sector signal. AI Labs importance 10 pts. Staleness 0 (assessed April 25).
top_entities.assessor_action: Floor-escalation protocol (Tier H4). Assess: (a) is composite 2.2 correctly expressing the minimum possible score under current methodology; (b) document the full evidence tier stack (10+ negative tiers: CSAM deepfakes, California C&D, French criminal probe, white supremacist content generation, Grok-1 data training consent violations, lack of safety infrastructure, lack of accountability mechanisms, conflict of interest via Musk's government role, antisemitic content generation, political manipulation); (c) apply permanent floor designation if evidence warrants. Note: xAI's positive indicators (Grok 3 model capability, open weights releases) should be assessed against this evidence stack to determine if any upward movement is warranted.

sector_alerts.alert: Netherlands two-tier asylum system passed Dutch Senate April 21 — family reunification denied to war/climate refugees. Combined with prior Norway age-sex protection ban (captured April 26 scan), Austria deportation hub participation, Germany deportation hub participation. Systemic legislative rollback of refugee rights across 4+ European countries within April 2026 window.

OpenAI Musk v. Altman trial Day 1 begins April 27 — breach of charitable trust + unjust enrichment claims proceed; OpenAI April 23 PBC conversion strips nonprofit of controlling stake. First major AI nonprofit accountability trial in history. Outcome could reshape AI governance scoring across entire AI Labs index.

sector_alerts.sector: AI Labs + F500 — Palantir Surveillance Scope Expansion
sector_alerts.alert: The Intercept April 24 reveals Palantir IRS $130M contract for massive-scale data mining across taxpayer records, ACA data, FinCEN data, dark web cryptocurrency data — scope extends from immigration surveillance (previously documented) into general population financial surveillance. Employee internal dissent ('descent into fascism') and activist office protests documented April 25. IRS contract is categorically distinct from ICE/immigration work and should be scored separately.

sector_alerts.sector: US States + US Cities — Immigration Enforcement Cascade
sector_alerts.alert: Texas SB4 activated April 24 (5th Circuit ruling) + Houston sanctuary ordinance gutted April 22 under $110M funding threat + ICE detention expansion protest April 24 (116,000 new beds planned across 24 new facilities) + Texas holds 18,734 ICE detainees (double any other state). A structural cascade from federal policy to state law to local policy coercion is documented in-window.

FAR clause 52.222-90 now required in all new federal contracts effective April 24-25. 30-day enforcement window runs through May 25. No entity-specific certification refusal or enforcement action confirmed for any F500 entity in April 25-27 window. Lockheed Martin compliance stated; Northrop Grumman compliance stated. Major defense contractors (Leidos, Raytheon, Northrop Grumman, Lockheed Martin) remain T2 watchlist — elevate to T1 if entity-specific FCA action confirmed by May 1.

sector_alerts.sector: Amnesty International 2026 Annual Report — Multi-Index Impact
sector_alerts.alert: Amnesty International 2026 Annual Report (April 20) issued institutional findings for: Israel (genocide + apartheid), Sudan (worst humanitarian crisis), DRC (M23 killings in Goma/Bukavu), Syria (massacres of Alawite/Druze communities), and the US (protest repression). Eight named countries listed for violent protest repression: Afghanistan, China, Egypt, India, Iran, Kenya, UK, USA, Venezuela. The report is an institutional-level evidence event that upgrades evidence quality for multiple entities in the pipeline.

methodological_notes: Norway and New Zealand: pending 04-26 proposals already capture the primary evidence. Not re-flagged. No genuinely new April 25-27 evidence for either entity beyond what is in the pending proposals.

The Netherlands two-tier asylum system (April 21 Senate passage) is the most significant single legislative event in today's scan — it converts a previously tracked policy intent into enacted law and directly removes refugee rights for a defined class. This is the primary driver of the Netherlands T1 elevation to rank 2.

OpenAI trial Day 1 (jury selection, April 27) is the primary new event. The April 23 PBC conversion memo was captured in the April 26 scan; the trial proceeding itself is the April 27 in-window event.

The Intercept's Palantir IRS $130M data mining story (published April 24) was within the April 26 scan window but was NOT captured in that scan's context flags or top entities (the April 26 scan focused on the EFF letter and congressional deadline). This is therefore new material for the April 27 scan.

South Sudan UNMISS: fifth consecutive deferral. The USUN explanation-of-vote page being indexed suggests the vote occurred but the press release date is not available in April 27 search results. Assessor must retrieve the actual vote terms.

Amnesty 2026 Annual Report (April 20) was captured in the April 26 scan for Netherlands context only. Today's scan expands its application to Israel, Sudan, DRC, and United States as an institutional-finding-level event.
Texas SB4 (April 24) and Houston ordinance reversal (April 22) are in-window and new — not captured in the April 26 scan. These elevate Texas (US States) to T1 for the first time.
EO 14398 implementation (April 24-25) is new in-window. No entity-specific F500 enforcement action confirmed — sector alert issued for DEI enforcement sprint watchlist.
